hints: A conversion factor equals $1$ only when the top and bottom describe the *same amount* in different units.
Is $1$ day really equal to $24$ hours? Then that fraction is $1$.
questions.explanation: $1$ day $= 24$ hours describes the same length of time, so $\dfrac{1 \text{ day}}{24 \text{ hours}} = 1$. The others mix up unrelated quantities (e.g., $1$ hour $\ne 24$ days; $100$ cm $\ne 1$ km).

questions.promptTex: \text{To convert } 5 \text{ hours into minutes, which factor do you multiply by?}
hints: You HAVE hours. You WANT minutes. Set up the factor so 'hours' is on the bottom (cancels) and 'minutes' is on the top (survives).
$5 \text{ hr} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$ — hours cancel, minutes survive.
questions.explanation: Put the unit you have (hours) on the bottom of the conversion factor so it cancels, and the unit you want (minutes) on top so it survives. $5 \,\cancel{\text{hr}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{hr}}} = 300 \text{ min}$.

hints: Multiply by $\dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hour}}$ so hours cancel.
$1 \text{ kg} = 1000 \text{ g}$, so multiply by $\dfrac{1 \text{ kg}}{1000 \text{ g}}$.

questions.promptTex: \text{A student writes:} \\ 4 \text{ km} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ km}}{1000 \text{ m}} = \dfrac{4 \,\text{km}^2}{1000 \,\text{m}} \\ \text{What went wrong?}
choices: They flipped the conversion factor; \text{km} should be on the bottom
hints: Look at the units in the answer: $\text{km}^2 / \text{m}$. That's nonsense — a sure sign of a wrong setup.
To convert km to m, you need km to *cancel*. So km goes on the bottom of the conversion factor.
questions.explanation: The student put km on the *top* of the conversion factor, so it didn't cancel — it stacked with the km from the original quantity, giving $\text{km}^2$. Correct setup: $4 \,\cancel{\text{km}} \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{km}}} = 4000 \text{ m}$. **Whenever units come out weird, the setup is wrong.**

hints: $1 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ m}$. Put km on the bottom of the conversion factor.

questions.promptTex: \text{To convert seconds to hours, which chain of factors works?}
hints: Walk through the cancellations on each option. The right one ends with hours surviving and everything else cancelled.
Each factor must cancel an unwanted unit and introduce a step toward 'hours'.
questions.explanation: Option B works: sec on the bottom of factor 1 (cancels with sec in the original quantity) and min on the bottom of factor 2 (cancels with the min introduced by factor 1). Only hours remain. Option A doesn't cancel anything — sec and min are both on top throughout.
steps: **Strategy.** Walk through each option and track which units cancel.

**Option A.** $\dfrac{60 \text{ sec}}{1 \text{ min}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$. Multiplied with the starting seconds, gives $\text{sec} \times \dfrac{\text{sec}}{\text{min}} \times \dfrac{\text{min}}{\text{hr}} = \dfrac{\text{sec}^2}{\text{hr}}$. Nonsense units.

**Option B.** $\dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ sec}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}$. Multiplied with the starting seconds: $\text{sec} \times \dfrac{\text{min}}{\text{sec}} \times \dfrac{\text{hr}}{\text{min}} = \text{hr}$. ✓
**Option C.** $\dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{3600 \text{ sec}^2}$ — the denominator has $\text{sec}^2$, which won't cancel cleanly with $\text{sec}$. Wrong.
**Option D.** $\dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ sec}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$. The min cancels, but ends in $\dfrac{\text{min}^2}{\text{hr}}$ — also nonsense.
**Pattern lesson.** When chaining, each factor in the chain must cancel the unit introduced by the previous factor and bring you one step closer to your target unit.

hints: Chain two conversion factors: $\dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ sec}}$ and $\dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}$.
steps: **Set up the chain.** Have seconds, want hours. Two conversion factors will bridge it: sec → min → hr.

**Arrange so units cancel.** Each factor's denominator must match the unit that needs to disappear. First factor: $\dfrac{1 \text{ min}}{60 \text{ sec}}$ (sec cancels). Second factor: $\dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}}$ (min cancels).
**Multiply through.** $7200 \times \dfrac{1}{60} \times \dfrac{1}{60} = \dfrac{7200}{3600} = 2$.
**Sanity check.** $2$ hours $\times$ $3600$ sec/hr $= 7200$ sec. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{A car travels at } 72 \text{ km/hr.} \\ \text{What is its speed in m/s?}
hints: Two conversions: km → m on the top, hr → s on the bottom.
$72 \times \dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ km}} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{3600 \text{ s}}$.
steps: **Recognize the type.** Compound unit (km/hr → m/s) requires two conversion factors — one for the top, one for the bottom.
**Cancel km.** The km is in the *top* of the original. To cancel, the conversion factor for km must have km in the *denominator*. $\dfrac{1000 \text{ m}}{1 \text{ km}}$ does it.
**Cancel hr.** The hr is in the *bottom* of the original. To cancel, the conversion factor for time must have hr in the *numerator*. $\dfrac{1 \text{ hr}}{3600 \text{ s}}$ does it.
**Multiply.** $\dfrac{72 \cdot 1000}{3600} = 20$. Units: $\dfrac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$.
**Pattern lesson.** Whenever you see X/Y units, expect *two* conversion factors — one to cancel X, one to cancel Y. Each one stacks independently.

questions.promptTex: \text{A flight travels } 4{,}500 \text{ km.} \\ \text{If the plane flies at } 900 \text{ km/hr,} \\ \text{how many MINUTES does the flight take?}
hints: First find the time in hours: $\text{time} = \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}}$.
Then convert hours to minutes by multiplying by $\dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$.
questions.explanation: Time in hours: $\dfrac{4500 \,\cancel{\text{km}}}{900 \,\cancel{\text{km}}/\text{hr}} = 5 \text{ hr}$. Convert to minutes: $5 \,\cancel{\text{hr}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{hr}}} = 300 \text{ min}$.
steps: **Set up.** Two-stage problem: first find time in hours, then convert to minutes.
**Stage 1 — time from distance & speed.** $\text{time} = \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \dfrac{4500 \text{ km}}{900 \text{ km/hr}}$.
**Cancel units in stage 1.** $\dfrac{\text{km}}{\text{km/hr}} = \text{km} \times \dfrac{\text{hr}}{\text{km}} = \text{hr}$. The km cancels — left with hours.
**Compute.** $4500/900 = 5$ hours.
**Stage 2 — convert to minutes.** $5 \,\cancel{\text{hr}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{hr}}} = 300$ min.
**Sanity check.** $300$ min $= 5$ hr. At $900$ km/hr for $5$ hr, distance is $4500$ km. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{If a runner can complete } 1 \text{ mile in } 6 \text{ minutes,} \\ \text{what is her pace in MILES PER HOUR?}
hints: Start by writing the pace as a fraction with units: $\dfrac{1 \text{ mile}}{6 \text{ min}}$.
Now convert the 'minutes' on the bottom into 'hours' by multiplying by $\dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$.
steps: **Set up.** The pace is $\dfrac{1 \text{ mile}}{6 \text{ min}}$. We want $\dfrac{\text{mile}}{\text{hour}}$ — so we need to convert the *denominator* from minutes to hours.

**Cancel min from the bottom.** Multiply by $\dfrac{60 \text{ min}}{1 \text{ hr}}$. The min cancels (one is on the bottom-bottom of a compound fraction, one is on the top of the new factor) and hr ends up on the bottom.
**Compute.** $\dfrac{1 \cdot 60}{6 \cdot 1} = 10$ mph.
**Sanity check.** $10$ mph means each mile takes $1/10$ of an hour $= 6$ minutes. ✓

questions.promptTex: \text{A water tap fills } 3 \text{ liters per second.} \\ \text{How many LITERS does it fill in } 1 \text{ hour}?
hints: Rate $\times$ time $=$ amount. But the units of time must match!
Convert $1$ hr to seconds first: $1 \text{ hr} \times \dfrac{3600 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ hr}} = 3600 \text{ s}$. Then multiply by the rate.
questions.explanation: $3 \,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\cancel{\text{s}}} \times 1 \,\cancel{\text{hr}} \times \dfrac{3600 \,\cancel{\text{s}}}{1 \,\cancel{\text{hr}}} = 3 \cdot 3600 = 10{,}800$ L. Or: in $1$ hour there are $3600$ seconds, each filling $3$ liters, so $3600 \cdot 3 = 10{,}800$ L.
steps: **Recognize the type.** Rate problem where the rate's time unit (sec) doesn't match the question's time unit (hr).
**Strategy.** Write everything as a chain of multiplications-by-$1$ that cancel into the desired units (here, just liters — no time at the end).

**Setup.** $3 \,\dfrac{\text{L}}{\text{s}} \times 1 \text{ hr} \times \dfrac{3600 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ hr}}$. The $1$ hr cancels with the hr in the new conversion factor; the new sec cancels with the sec in the rate's denominator.
**Surviving units.** Only L remains. Good.
**Multiply.** $3 \times 3600 = 10{,}800$ liters.

**Pattern lesson.** Whenever a rate is given in one time unit and the question asks in another, *don't try to convert the rate first* — just chain factors so the unwanted time unit cancels. Less error-prone.
